comments:
Summary and strengths:
- The paper applies convolutional deep networks to smile (AU12) recognition on the DISFA database and reports a very high binary classification accuracy (99.45%), substantially higher than previously reported SVM-based baselines (65.55%–79.67%).
- The work includes a model selection procedure over architecture choices (number of conv layers, hidden layers, units, dropout) and compares mouth-only and full-face inputs. Training on a GPU to enable more extensive search is sensible and practically useful.

Major concerns and weaknesses:
1) Evaluation methodology likely optimistic due to data splitting. The paper states a random 60/20/20 split of images but does not indicate subject-independent splitting. Because DISFA consists of video frames from a small number of subjects, a random frame-wise split allows frames of the same subject (and very similar frames) into both train and test, inflating performance. A subject-disjoint split is standard for fair assessment but is not reported.
2) Very limited reporting of metrics. Only overall classification rate is reported. No per-class precision/recall, ROC/AUC, confusion matrix, or per-intensity performance (DISFA provides intensities) is shown. With class imbalance and practical considerations, accuracy alone is insufficient.
3) Model selection procedure is simplified and incomplete. Parameters were optimized independently (one-at-a-time), which can miss interactions between hyperparameters. Many other choices were fixed (learning rate, momentum, filter sizes, number feature maps) without justification or sensitivity analysis.
4) Reproducibility and detail gaps. Exact architectures (numbers of filters per layer, activation details beyond ReLU, weight initialization, weight decay, data augmentation, preprocessing normalization) are not fully described. Batch size and optimizer are given but other training details (early stopping rules, rationale for training 1000 epochs) are sparse.
5) Statistical claims appear suspect. The reported standard deviation of test accuracy across similar experiments is 0.041725% — an implausibly small value — and the experiments were run once due to this claim. More robust estimation (multiple random seeds/splits or cross-validation) is needed to support the high reported accuracy.
6) Comparison to prior work is limited. The baselines cited are older SVM/hand-crafted-feature results (from DISFA paper). No comparison is made to other modern deep-learning approaches on the same database or to alternative preprocessing (e.g., using landmark-normalized inputs) to isolate the source of the large performance gap.
7) Possible overfitting and generalization concerns. Training for up to 1000 epochs with very high accuracy suggests potential overfitting; no training/validation curves or regularization analysis are provided beyond dropout selection.

Minor comments and suggestions:
- Report subject-independent splits and/or cross-validation to establish realistic generalization performance.
- Provide class-wise metrics (precision, recall, F1), ROC/AUC, and confusion matrices; report results per AU intensity to demonstrate robustness across expression strengths.
- Clarify dataset preprocessing, alignment, normalization, and whether adjacent frames were deduplicated in train/test.
- Provide full architecture details (filters per layer, number of parameters), training curves, and variance over multiple runs with different random seeds.
- Compare to more recent learning-based baselines where available, and include an ablation study (impact of depth, number of conv layers, data augmentation).
- Release code and model weights to aid reproducibility.

Recommendation rationale:
The application of CNNs to smile detection is sensible and the reported numbers are impressive, but substantial methodological shortcomings prevent confidence in the claimed performance and conclusions. The paper would be strengthened by subject-disjoint evaluation, richer metrics, clearer experimental detail, and more rigorous statistical reporting. Given these issues, I view the paper as having moderate interest but in need of revision before stronger endorsement.
